title: Systematic Review of Accountability in Pakistan's Polio Eradication Program
author: Imre Papp

steps.title: Systematically search five databases
steps.instruction: Systematically search five databases: Embase - Embase via Ovid , PubMed , SSCI - Social Science Citation Index, Scopus, and EconLit.

steps.title: Use search terms related to
steps.instruction: Use search terms related to: a) Pakistan (including province names) AND b) corruption or accountability or governance or integrity AND c) polio or poliomyelitis or "polio vaccine"

steps.title: If satisfied with search results, save...
steps.instruction: If satisfied with search results, save search string and record date of search. Download RIS file of publications.

steps.title: If not satisfied, refine search and...
steps.instruction: If not satisfied, refine search and reassess before recording search string.

steps.title: Upload RIS files containing search results...
steps.instruction: Upload RIS files containing search results to Covidence software [1] for review.

steps.title: Covidence automatically removes most duplicates
steps.instruction: Covidence automatically removes most duplicates. If some remain, remove manually.

steps.title: Assess eligibility based on title relevance...
steps.instruction: Assess eligibility based on title relevance according to inclusion and exclusion criteria. Two independent reviewers complete the screening process (steps 6-8) and must reach consensus. If there is disagreement regarding the eligibility of a study at any point in the screening process (steps 6-8), the reviewers should discuss together and reach an agreement.

steps.title: Inclusion criteria
steps.instruction: Inclusion criteria: research took place in Pakistan, is related to a polio eradication program, and addresses program efficacy/accountability/corruption. Written in English language, published between 2000 and 2026. Exclusion criteria: research not in Pakistan or relating to the polio program, studies assessing the efficacy of the vaccine itself (and not the delivery program), articles not related to the success/limitations of eradication efforts. Not written in English language, or published before 2000.

steps.title: Repeat step 6, this time assessing...
steps.instruction: Repeat step 6, this time assessing abstract relevance to determine potential eligibility.

steps.title: Conduct a full-text review of remaining studies to determine eligibility
steps.instruction: Conduct a full-text review of remaining studies to determine eligibility.

steps.title: While conducting full-text reviews, look through...
steps.instruction: While conducting full-text reviews, look through citations for any further potentially relevant articles.

steps.title: Finally, search WHO, UNICEF, World Bank,...
steps.instruction: Finally, search WHO, UNICEF, World Bank, and Global Polio Eradication Initiative (GPEI) database for any grey literature surrounding polio governance, accountability, and/or corruption.

steps.title: Import articles into analysis software (i
steps.instruction: Import articles into analysis software (i.e., Quirkos) to facilitate systematic coding and organization of data.

steps.title: Analyze data using Thomas and Harden's thematic synthesis approach [2]
steps.instruction: Analyze data using Thomas and Harden's thematic synthesis approach [2]. First, conduct line-by-line coding on the findings sections of each included study, generating codes inductively from the data. Coding is to be conducted iteratively, with initial codes refined and expanded as new studies are incorporated.

steps.title: Codes should then be grouped into...
steps.instruction: Codes should then be grouped into descriptive themes that capture recurring patterns across studies. A constant comparative approach should be used to examine similarities and differences within and across studies, ensuring that themes remain grounded in the primary data.

steps.title: Analytical themes can then be developed...
steps.instruction: Analytical themes can then be developed through interpretive analysis, moving beyond the original study findings to generate higher-order insights related to the research question. The development of analytical themes involves ongoing discussion among the research team to challenge assumptions and ensure coherence. To enhance the credibility of the synthesis, illustrative participant quotes will be used to support key themes, and an audit trail of analytical decisions will be maintained throughout the process.

steps.title: year of publication,
steps.instruction: year of publication,

steps.title: country and setting of study,
steps.instruction: country and setting of study,

steps.title: inclusion criteria, 10) # of participants,
steps.instruction: inclusion criteria, 10) # of participants,

steps.title: identified themes/subthemes (for qualitative work),
steps.instruction: identified themes/subthemes (for qualitative work),

steps.title: important quotes (for qualitative work),
steps.instruction: important quotes (for qualitative work),

steps.title: key findings/results summary,
steps.instruction: key findings/results summary,

steps.title: Assess methodological quality and confidence in...
steps.instruction: Assess methodological quality and confidence in the findings using the GRADE or GRADE-CERQual approach, for quantitative and qualitative findings, respectively [3,4]. The methodological quality of included qualitative studies will be assessed using the Critical Appraisal Skills Programme (CASP) Qualitative Checklist. Each study will be evaluated across domains including study design, data collection, reflexivity, ethical considerations, and rigor of analysis. Quality appraisal will be conducted independently by at least two reviewers, with disagreements resolved through discussion.

steps.title: Each review finding will be evaluated across four domains
steps.instruction: Each review finding will be evaluated across four domains: Methodological limitations: the extent to which there are concerns about the design or conduct of the primary studies contributing to a finding Coherence: the degree to which the finding is well grounded in and supported by the data across studies Adequacy of data: the richness and quantity of data supporting the finding Relevance: the applicability of the data to the review question and context Each finding will be assigned an overall level of confidence (high, moderate, low, or very low), and a summary of qualitative findings table will be produced.
